titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the James Henry Burton Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Staff of Manuscripts and Archives

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday September 2, 2020 at 08:40

did.unittitle: James Henry Burton papers
did.abstract: Correspondence, diaries, notebooks, clippings, printed matter and a draft history of the Richmond Armory which he headed from 1861 to 1862. Included also are notes on machinery and a British patent for small fire-arms.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: James Henry Burton Papers (MS 117). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Edwin Pugsley, 1953, 1958, and 1962. Gift of Eugenia Scheffel, 1998.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: Correspondence and other papers available on microfilm (2 reels, 35mm.) from Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library, at cost. Order no. HM3.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Colonel James H. Burton Papers consist of correspondence, diaries, notebooks, photographs, plans, and miscellaneous items spanning from 1845 to 1950.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Colonel James H. Burton (1823-1894), after he had been educated at Westchester Academy, Pennsylvania, entered a Baltimore machine shop at the age of sixteen. In 1844, he took employment in the rifle works of the United States armory at Harper's Ferry, was appointed foreman in 1845 and master armorer shortly thereafter. In 1855, he accepted the post of chief engineer of the Royal Small Arms Factory, Enfield, near London, and did not return to Virginia until 1861. In June of that year, Burton was commissioned a Lieutenant Colonel in the Ordinance Department of the Virginia State Militia and placed in charge of the State Armory by Governor John Letcher with instructions to arrange for the removal of all machinery captured at Harper's Ferry and place it in position for use. Within ninety days, Colonel Burton had the machinery at work in Richmond producing rifles of standard United States patterns.

The following September, Burton was appointed superintendent of arms by President Jefferson Davis with the rank of Lieutenant Colonel. During the summer of 1863, the Confederate Government ordered Burton to Europe on State Department business. He did not return to the United States for three years and only then after having received an amnesty from President Johnson.
As a private citizen, he returned to England in 1871 to take direction of a Leeds firm manufacturing arms for the Russian Government. He returned to Virginia in 1873.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright status for collection materials is unknown, though much of the material in this collection is likely in the public domain. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Parts of the papers were microfilmed in 1970 and 1974. The papers were reorganized in 1979 and no longer correspond with the film.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The collection is open for research.

did.unittitle: Notebooks
Miscellaneous
Scrapbook
Photographs of James Burton, the Macon Armory, and unidentified persons and buildings
Newspaper clippings
James Hall: "Report upon the Property of the Empire State Iron and Coal Company, of Georgia"
Correspondence concerning James Burton
Notes concerning James Burton, machinery, and related subjects
Frank E. Vandiver: "A Sketch of Efforts Abroad to Equip the Confederate Armory at Macon," Georgia Historical Quarterly
British patent issued for an improvement in the manufacture of barrels for small fire-arms
Plans, drawings, and maps
Confederate States Armory, Macon, Georgia
Front elevation or view from Macon & Western Railroad, 21" x 98"
Design of Dwelling House for Mr. Herrington Mast. Machinist, 30 1/4" x 34 1/2"
Design of cast iron ventilator for front building & wings, James A. Burton, Supt., 13 1/4" x 30 1/4"
Ground plan of basement story. Central Office, 10" x 14 3/4"
Map of Southwestern Part of City of Macon
Shows location of Confedrate States Armory and proposed connection of Armory track with Macon & Western Railroad signed A. Schwaab, CE, 14 3/4" x 18"
Shows land purchased for Confederate States Armory by Col. Burton, Superintendent Surveyed by A. Schwaab, Civ. Eng., 23" x 24"
Shows land purchased for Confederate States Armory by Col. Burton, Superintendent Surveyed by A. Schwaab, Civ. Eng., 21" x 22 3/4"
Shows location of Confederate States Armory and proposed connexion of Armory Track with Macon & Western Railroad. Aug. Schwaab, CE, 15" x 16"
Alteration of 2 screw drilling machines for facing striker nut (+B rifle) Order 528B full size, 26" x 39"
General Store House 202 (ft.)/8 in. x 40 ft. 2 Stories High, 16 1/2" x 39"
Cast iron base of column for main building of new armory. Jas. A. Burton, Supt. C. S., Armory, Macon, Ga., 22 3/4" x 30 1/4"
Design arrangement of lightning conductors (labeled on verso: General ground plans of roofs B), 22" x 29"
General Ground Plan of roofs of Confederate States Armory to be erected in the city of Macon, made for the purpose of (labeled on verso: General ground plans of roofs A), 26 1/2" x 29"
Untitled (labeled on verso: #3), 29 1/2" x 36 1/2"
Untitled, 3 pieces of one floor plan, (labeled on verso: 1, 2, 3), 1 - 29" x 36 1/2", 2 & 3 - 30" x 36 1/2"
Vertical cross - nos - Main Work Shops, Detail of roof ridge, 19 3/4" x 28 3/4"
Confederate States Armory at Macon, GA. General Groundplan, 3 pieces, 1 - 22" x 39", 2 - 30" x 40", 3 -30" x 39
